actions.thought: I am in the Small Blind/Button with J2o, which is a very weak hand (bottom 20% of ranges). Although heads-up ranges are wide and I would have position post-flop, J2o has poor playability and equity. Opening this hand is generally -EV, and limping exposes me to isolation raises. Since it is the first hand and I have no reads on the opponent, the standard and disciplined play is to fold.

In a heads-up match, Ace-high is a very strong hand on the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent a strong range. This is a standard opening move to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage.

In a heads-up match, KJo is a strong hand (top 15-20% of hands). Facing a standard-ish 3bb open from the Button (who should be opening very wide, often 80%+ of hands), I am well ahead of their range. I am out of position post-flop. 3-betting is an option, but it often folds out the hands I dominate (like K2-K9, QJ, JTs) and isolates me against better hands or flips. Calling allows me to realize my equity against a wide range and keeps the pot manageable while deep-stacked. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 25% equity vs my actual ~60%). Therefore, I will call.

I have Top Pair with a good kicker (Kings with Jack) on a Kh-8h-4s board. In heads-up play, this is a premium hand. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard and most profitable play is to check. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including weaker Kings, pocket pairs, flush draws, or complete air. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against their value range. I intend to check and call a standard bet to keep their bluffs in the pot.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on a King-high board. I hold the Ace of Hearts, which gives me a backdoor nut flush draw and an overcard to the board. By betting 300 (half the pot), I am representing a strong King and forcing my opponent to fold hands that missed this board entirely. If called, I still have equity to improve on the turn.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a strong kicker (Jack) on a board of Kh-8h-4s. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) from the Button, my hand is very strong relative to their wide heads-up range. Check-raising is an option to charge draws, but calling is generally superior here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's bluffs in the pot, allowing them to fire again on the turn. 2) It protects my checking range. 3) It avoids isolating myself against hands that beat me (AK, KQ, sets) or getting into a bloated pot out of position this deep (96bb). I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board is Kh-8h-4s-9c. The 9c is a relatively safe card, though it connects with some random 9x or specifically K9/98/T7 hands. I still hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KJ). In a heads-up scenario, this hand remains very strong. My opponent (Flash) bet the flop, and I called. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) here is suboptimal because it denies the opponent the chance to bluff with missed draws (hearts) or weaker hands they might try to represent. It also folds out their air which I beat. By checking, I induce them to fire a second barrel with semi-bluffs or value bet worse hands (like KT). If they check back, I can comfortably value bet the river. The plan is to check and call a reasonable bet.

I have Ace-high with the nut flush blocker on a board where the opponent called a flop continuation bet. The 9c on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since Pro checked again, they might be holding a marginal made hand like an 8 or a 4, or a drawing hand. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, realize my showdown value, and avoid getting check-raised off my hand while still having the chance to hit an Ace on the river.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a decent kicker, but the River card (Th) is very dangerous. It completes the heart flush draw (3 hearts on board) and creates a 4-card straight possibility (K-T-9-8 on board, requiring QJ for the straight). My opponent checked back the turn, which usually caps their range to weak Kings, marginal pairs, or draws. Since the obvious draws (flush and straight) have now completed, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like mid-pairs) and will simply pay off better hands. By checking, I allow the opponent to check back with weaker Kings or pairs that I beat. I avoid opening myself up to a raise and can evaluate if they decide to bet, though the board texture heavily favors their checking-back range improving.

On this river, the Ten of Hearts completes a potential flush and some straights. I hold the Ace of Hearts, which is the nut flush blocker. While this makes me a good candidate to bluff, I have already checked the turn, and my opponent has checked to me twice. I have some showdown value with Ace-high against missed lower draws, and checking back allows me to realize that value without risking a check-raise from a trapped hand or a better King-high. I will check to see the showdown.

I am holding 6c 2s, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (62 offsuit). Although I have the Button (which acts as the Small Blind in Heads-Up) and will have position post-flop, the equity of this hand is too low to justify entering the pot, even for a limp. Conserving chips is better than playing trash hands this early in the tournament.

Qs 8s is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button, I want to take control of the pot and use my positional advantage. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 93s facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. While heads-up requires wide defense, 93s is a bottom-tier hand. The pot odds are 2:1 (requiring 33% equity), but my equity realization out of position with such a disconnected low hand is poor. Against a 3BB raise, this is a fold; I would only consider defending vs a min-raise.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with K3o in a heads-up match. With 100BB deep stacks, K3o is a marginal hand that is often dominated. Standard heads-up ranges usually fold the weakest off-suit Kings (K2o-K4o) while playing K5o+ and suited Kings. While I have position, without a specific read that the opponent over-folds his Big Blind, simply completing or raising is slightly -EV or high variance. Folding allows me to wait for better opportunities early in the match.

I am on the button with 6s 3s. While this is a weak hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression and opening a wide range from the button is standard strategy. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to try and take down the blinds or play the hand with initiative post-flop.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. As the Big Blind, I am getting pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring roughly 33% equity to call. My hand, 89o, is a reasonable connector in a Heads-Up match, but it suffers significantly from being offsuit and out of position. Against a standard min-raise (2bb), this would be a defend, but the opponent's larger 3x sizing reduces my pot odds and makes it harder to realize equity post-flop. Given the 3bb open, 89o falls slightly below the profitable defense threshold due to the difficulty of playing it OOP. I will fold to preserve my stack.

In Heads-Up play, Q8o is a slightly above-average hand (top 42%). I have folded my previous button opportunities (Hand 1, 3, and 5) with weaker holdings. To avoid becoming too predictable and to capitalize on position and hand strength, I should open the pot. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate to assert initiative and potentially take down the blinds pre-flop or play the hand in position.

I am in the big blind holding 72 suited. My opponent on the button has min-raised to 200. Although the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a pot of 300), 72 is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing such a low-equity hand out of position throughout the rest of the street is generally a losing strategy against a competent opponent. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I have a pocket pair (2h 2c) on the button. In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I have noticed 'Pro' folding frequently to pre-flop raises, so I will continue with my standard 3x open to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 7h facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent has shown a tendency to open 3x frequently. While heads-up requires defending wider, 75o is at the very bottom of the playable range. Pot odds are 33% (200 to win 600), and 75o has roughly 32-35% equity against a wide opening range. However, realizing that equity out of position in a raised pot is difficult with an unconnected off-suit hand. Against a 3x sizing specifically, this is a standard fold.

In Heads-Up play, A7 suited is a strong hand, well above the median starting hand strength. As the dealer (SB), I have position post-flop. The standard play is to raise to seize the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or build a pot with a hand that plays well (flush potential and ace blocker). I choose a min-raise to 200, which offers a good risk-to-reward ratio and balances my opening range.

I am in the big blind with 9d 6d. Pro has min-raised from the button to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 400 total. The pot odds are 3:1, which is excellent for a suited hand with some connectivity. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on how it hits my range.

I have missed the flop completely with 9d 6d on a board of 8s-Js-Ad. This board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which likely includes many Aces, Jacks, and spade draws. Since I have no pair and no meaningful draw, I will check and plan to fold to most bets.

I have flopped Top Pair (Aces) on a texture that favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor (Ad Js 8s). While the kicker (7) is mediocre, it is strong enough to bet for value in a heads-up pot. The board is somewhat wet with a flush draw (two spades) and straight draws possible, so betting serves to extract value from worse pairs (Jx, 8x) and charge draws. Checking back is an option for pot control, but allowing a free card on a flush-draw board risks giving infinite odds to draws. A small c-bet (approx 35-40% of pot) keeps the opponent's range wide while getting value.

I have 9-high with no pair and no draw on a board of 8s-Js-Ad. This board texture is very strong for the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Aces and Jacks. My opponent has bet 150 into a 400 pot. Since I have nearly zero equity and no reasonable way to win the hand without a massive bluff on a board that favors my opponent, I will fold.

In a heads-up match, a suited Ten is a reasonable hand to play aggressively from the small blind. My previous raises to 3x (300 total) have been successful in forcing folds from my opponent in hands 4, 6, and 8. I will continue this pattern of aggression to try and win the blinds or see a flop with the initiative.

I hold 6c 2h in the Big Blind, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker (low, unsuited, disconnected). Although I am getting pot odds of 3:1 (requiring ~33% equity), my raw equity against a reasonable opening range is poor, and the playability of this hand out of position is extremely low. The opponent has been consistently opening to 300, making the price to call less attractive than a min-raise. Defending here would be a mistake; folding is the standard play.

In a heads-up match, K3s is a respectable starting hand from the button (Small Blind), ranking well within the top 50% of hands. It has both high card value and flush potential. Given the opponent's history of folding to aggression when out of position (Hand 7 and 9), raising is superior to limping or folding. A minimum raise to 200 puts pressure on the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable, allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button/Small Blind has raised to 200 chips. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given that it is still early in the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots rather than defending with a bottom-tier hand.

I have Ace-Nine offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand and I have been successfully applying pressure with 3x raises in previous hands. I will continue this strategy to take control of the pot pre-flop.

The opponent opens to 300 (3bb) from the Button. This 3x sizing worsens my pot odds compared to a standard min-raise, requiring roughly 33% equity to call. While my hand T3s has around 40% raw equity against a wide opening range, my realization of that equity out of position with a gapped, low-kicker hand is poor. I have folded similar weak hands to this specific sizing previously. Against a 3x open, T3s falls outside the profitable defense range due to the difficulty of playing post-flop.

I hold 2s 6c (Six-Deuce offsuit), which is effectively the weakest hand in Hold'em. Despite being on the Button (SB) and having position post-flop in a heads-up match, the hand has negligible equity and poor playability. My opponent has folded to raises before, but risking chips on a pure bluff with the absolute bottom of my range is unnecessary at this stage (Hand 13/150). Folding is the disciplined and standard decision here.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit, which is a solid hand to open in heads-up play. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to aggression pre-flop or early on the flop in previous hands. I will continue my pattern of opening to 3x the big blind (300) to put pressure on the big blind and try to take down the pot early or play in position.

My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the button every single time they have had the position so far (Hands 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14), indicating a very wide, possibly 100% opening range. While I need to start defending wider to prevent being exploited, 4s5c (45o) is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Even against a random hand, 45o only has about 32% equity, and realizing that equity OOP is difficult. The price of calling 200 to win a total pot of 600 offers 33% pot odds, which is borderline for raw equity but poor considering playability. I will wait for a slightly better hand (suited connectors, any high card, or connected mid-cards) to make a stand against this aggression.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 8c 5c, a suited two-gapper. In heads-up play, this hand is well within the playable range to open, having roughly 43% equity against a random hand and playability post-flop due to flush and straight potential. My opponent 'Flash' has folded to my previous min-raises (Hands 7, 11) or folded on the flop (Hand 9), suggesting they are not defending the Big Blind overly aggressively against my opens. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or utilize my position post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is consistent with my previous winning strategy.

AQo is a premium hand in heads-up play. Since Pro has raised from the button, I will 3-bet to 600 to build the pot for value and gain initiative. This sizing puts pressure on the button's wider opening range while allowing me to extract value from dominated hands.

I hold 8c 5c on the button and faced a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind after my initial min-raise. While I am in position and the pot odds require roughly 33% equity (calling 400 to win 1200), 8c 5c is a speculative hand that is at the bottom of my opening range. Against a standard or value-heavy 3-bet range (which is likely given this is the first 3-bet from the opponent in 15 hands), my equity is likely around 30-34%. Without a read that the opponent is 3-betting light, defending this specific hand is -EV. I should defend with better suited connectors, pairs, and broadways. Folding preserves chips and respects the opponent's aggression.

In a heads-up tournament setting, J3 suited is a strong enough hand to open from the Small Blind (Button). I have been raising to 300 consistently in this position, and given the blinds of 50/100, this sizing is standard to put pressure on the Big Blind. I will continue this strategy to maintain control of the table and capitalize on my positional advantage.

I hold Pocket Queens, a premium hand. The opponent has been raising aggressively from the button (3x opens), indicating a wide range. Being out of position with a strong hand, a 3-bet is the standard play to extract value, negate positional disadvantage, and charge the opponent's wide range. Calling is an option to trap, but 3-betting builds the pot immediately and protects against overcards on the flop. A raise to 1000 (roughly 3.3x the open) is an appropriate size for being out of position with deep stacks.

I opened with Jack-Trey suited from the button, which is a standard play in heads-up to maintain pressure. Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While I am in position, J3s is near the bottom of my opening range and does not play well against a 3-bet range that likely contains better Jacks, high pairs, and strong Aces. Calling 700 more would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak hand in a bloated pot. Given the tournament context and my stack size, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold T9 suited on the Button (SB), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up poker due to its playability and potential to make straights and flushes. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to min-raises in previous hands (Hands 7, 11). Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play a pot in position with a hand that connects well with many boards. A min-raise to 200 is a standard and effective size here.

I am holding K9o in the Big Blind. Pro has opened for 200 from the Button. It costs me 100 more to call into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. K9o is well above the threshold for a standard defense in this position against a Button open. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop, as K9o has decent high-card value and I am getting a great price to continue.

The flop of Ad-6c-7c significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as they are more likely to have an Ace. I have King high and no direct draws to the current board. I will check to the opponent to see how they proceed. This is a standard check-fold or check-evaluate situation given I have missed the flop completely.

I hold Td9d on a board of Ad-6c-7c. I have a gutshot straight draw (need an 8) and a backdoor flush draw. While the Ace favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, the 6 and 7 connect well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity. By checking back, I can realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and potentially improve to a straight or pair, or pick up a flush draw. It also balances my checking range.

On the turn 3c, the board now shows three clubs. I have the 9 of clubs, providing a flush draw, and King-high. My opponent (Pro) has checked for a second time, suggesting they likely do not have a strong Ace or a completed flush. While I could bet to represent the flush or an Ace, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially win at showdown with King-high or hit a club on the river. Given the tournament context and the fact that I have some showdown value, checking is a low-variance play.

The turn is the 3c, making the board [Ad 6c 7c 3c]. This is a monotone club board. I hold [Td 9d], which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), but I do not hold a club. My opponent defended the Big Blind, meaning their range contains many suited hands and random clubs. Checking back the flop on an Ace-high board capped my range somewhat, and the arrival of the third club makes the board very wet and dangerous. Betting here is risky because I have no club blocker and very little equity if called; my opponent will likely continue with any club, any pair, or a better Ace. Checking allows me to realize my straight equity for free and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand. If I hit an 8 on the river, I make a straight (though a flush is still a threat). If I miss, I can easily fold or re-evaluate.

The board has paired Aces and contains three clubs. I have King-high with the 9 of clubs blocker. Both players have checked the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand like an Ace or a flush. While I could attempt to bluff, King-high has a small amount of showdown value against missed straight draws or lower high cards (like Queen-high). I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if the opponent makes a significant bet, as it is unlikely I can represent a strong enough hand to fold out a pair at this point in such a small pot.

On the River, the board is [Ad 6c 7c 3c Ah]. The pot is 400. I hold [Td 9d], which is Ten-high and has zero showdown value. My opponent, Flash, has checked to me on the Flop, Turn, and now River. This extreme passivity strongly suggests he does not have a strong Ace or a Flush, as he likely would have bet the Turn or River for value or protection. His range is capped at weak pairs (6x, 7x, 3x) or missed draws/high cards. The arrival of the second Ace on the river improves my range perception, as I can credibly represent a slow-played weak Ace that has now made trips, or a flush that decided to bet the river. Given my opponent's history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hand 9, Hand 16), a half-pot bet should generate enough fold equity to make this a profitable bluff against his weak, capped range.

On the river, the board has paired Aces and there are three clubs present. I have King-high with the 9 of clubs blocker. Pro checked the flop and turn, which usually indicates they do not have a strong Ace or a completed flush. However, Pro's bet of 200 into 400 on the river, following the second Ace appearing, suggests they might have a small pair or a weak Ace that they are now betting for value. While the pot odds are 3:1, King-high is rarely the best hand when an opponent leads into the pot on the river after a passive line. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In heads-up play, 8-5 offsuit is a weak hand, but it is standard to maintain aggression from the button. I have been consistently opening for 300 (3x) from this position throughout the match. I will continue this pattern to put pressure on the big blind, intending to fold if faced with a significant 3-bet.

I hold KTo in the Big Blind, facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button. KTo is a strong heads-up hand, well ahead of a random hand and competitive against a standard button opening range. The opponent has been opening frequently (Hands 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, etc.), indicating a wide range. Getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), I need 33% equity, which KTo easily surpasses (approx. 58% vs random). While a 3-bet is possible, calling keeps the opponent's range wide and manages the pot size out of position.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a board of Kd 6d 9c. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet (c-bet) with a wide range, including bluffs and draws, which I can profitably call. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his weaker hands that I want to keep in the pot. My hand is strong enough to check-call comfortably.

The flop of Kd-9c-6d is quite favorable for me as the pre-flop aggressor, as it hits my range harder than it typically hits the Big Blind's calling range. Since Pro checked, I will follow up with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to put pressure on their marginal hands and represent top pair or better.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a [Kd 6d 9c] board. The opponent, who has been aggressive pre-flop, continues with a half-pot c-bet of 300. My hand is strong, beating many of the opponent's value hands (lower Kings, pocket pairs below King, 9x) and all of their bluffs. Raising here might fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot or isolate me against better Kings (KQ, AK). Calling is the standard play to control the pot size out of position and allow the opponent to potentially fire another barrel on the turn with weaker holdings or draws. I am getting 3:1 pot odds which makes this an easy call.

On the turn [2d], the board now has three diamonds. I hold Top Pair (Kings) and have improved equity with the [Td] providing a flush draw. Despite the strength of my hand, leading out is not advisable as it folds out the opponent's bluffs and isolates me against better hands like completed flushes or sets. Checking is the standard line out of position to control the pot size and allow the opponent to either bluff or check back with a weaker hand. If he bets, my hand is strong enough to continue, but for now, checking is the correct decision.

The turn brings the Deuce of Diamonds, completing the flush draw that was present on the flop. After my flop continuation bet was called, I am in a difficult spot with 8-high and no equity. Pro's check on the turn could indicate a trap with a completed flush or a King that is now playing cautiously. Since I have no showdown value and no draws, checking back is the most sensible play to control the pot size. This allows me to see the river for free and potentially evaluate a bluff on a non-diamond river if Pro checks again, or simply concede the hand if I don't improve.

The river brings the 3d, putting four diamonds on the board [Kd 6d 2d 3d]. I hold the Td, giving me a flush (K-T-6-3-2). While strong, it is not the nuts; I lose to any player holding the Ad, Qd, or Jd. The opponent checked back the turn, which suggests he likely lacks a premium hand, though a trapped high flush draw is possible. Betting here is thin for value: worse flushes (9d, 8d, etc.) might call, but better flushes will certainly call or raise, and non-flush hands will simply fold. By checking, I control the pot size against better hands and allow the opponent to potentially bluff with air or check back a worse hand that has showdown value. Checking is the disciplined play on a 4-flush board out of position with a non-nut flush.

The board has run out with four diamonds (6d-9c-Kd-2d-3d). I currently hold 8-high with no diamonds, meaning I have zero showdown value and lose to any hand containing a diamond, any pair, and even lower high-card hands that might have stayed in. Pro has checked the river after calling my flop continuation bet and checking the turn. While a bluff could potentially force a fold from a King or a Nine without a diamond, the presence of four diamonds makes it highly likely that Pro holds at least a low diamond or will be suspicious of a bluff given I checked the turn. Since the turn and river both brought diamonds and I have no blockers, I will check behind and concede the pot to minimize further losses.

I hold Queen-Jack offsuit on the Button (Small Blind), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the median. My strategy is to raise to take the initiative and utilize my position post-flop. A minimum raise to 200 is my standard opening size, offering a good balance of pot-building while keeping the risk low if re-raised.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button raised to 200, which is a standard opening size. To call, I only need to put in 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400 total. Getting 4:1 pot odds, I have a sufficient hand to defend my big blind against a wide button opening range. A fold is too tight here, and a raise is unnecessary given the hand strength.

I am first to act on a flop of 2s-Ad-7d after defending my big blind pre-flop. I have King-high with no pair and very little potential to improve, other than a backdoor diamond draw. Since the board contains an Ace, which hits the pre-flop raiser's range frequently, I will check and see how my opponent reacts. I do not have enough hand strength to lead into the raiser here.

The flop comes [2s Ad 7d]. As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace heavily favors my perceived range compared to the Big Blind's calling range. In a previous hand (Hand #9), the opponent folded to a continuation bet on an Ace-high board. Although I only have Queen-high, betting here allows me to represent an Ace, deny equity to random overcards or floats, and potentially fold out better hands like small pocket pairs or weak 7x/2x. A small bet size of roughly 1/3 pot (150) is standard for this texture to accomplish these goals cheaply.

The flop of Ad-7d-2s is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range. My King-high has very little showdown value here and almost no potential to improve to the best hand, especially since any King that hits might still be second-best to Pro's Ace-King or pocket pairs. Even though the bet size of 150 into 400 is relatively small, calling with K3 offsuit on an Ace-high board is generally a losing play over time. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with Ace-Nine offsuit (As 9c), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Based on previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has been willing to call raises with relatively weak hands (like 85o in Hand #18) but has also folded to pressure. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size from the button and allows me to build a pot with a superior hand while maintaining positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 2c facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. The opponent has been opening this size frequently, suggesting a wide range. However, 72s is one of the weakest starting hands. Pot odds require 33% equity to call (200 to win a total pot of 600), and while my raw equity against a wide range is close to this, realizing that equity out of position with such a weak hand is extremely difficult. The 3x sizing makes the price too expensive for a speculative defend with the bottom of my range. I will fold.

I hold A2s on the button, which is a strong hand in heads-up play (top 30% of hands). Given the opponent's tendency to fold to raises or play straightforwardly, raising is the best option to build the pot and seize initiative. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200 chips.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise from 'Pro'. ATo is a premium hand in heads-up play and is well ahead of a standard button opening range. Looking at previous hands, 'Pro' has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets (Hand #15) and c-bets. By raising to 600, I take initiative, build a pot with a strong hand, and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I have a hand that flops well.

I hold A2s on the button and faced a 3-bet from the Big Blind to 600. The pot is 800 and I need to call 400, offering me 33% pot odds. A2s is a solid hand heads-up with good playability post-flop due to flush and straight potential, plus the blocker value of the Ace. Being in position with deep stacks (over 80 BB) makes calling the 3-bet the most attractive option to realize my equity rather than folding or turning it into a 4-bet bluff this early. I will call to see a flop.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Ten and was called by Pro. The flop of Jack-Four-Three is relatively dry and favors my perceived 3-betting range, which includes strong Jacks, overpairs (QQ-AA), and big overcards. By leading with a continuation bet of 400 (1/3 of the pot), I maintain the initiative and put pressure on Pro's marginal hands like middle pairs or broadway cards that missed. Given that Pro has folded to aggression in several previous hands (Hand #9, #11, #16, #19), a standard C-bet is the most effective way to take down the pot now.

I am holding As2s on a board of 3c 4d Jc. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 5) and an Ace overcard. The opponent 3-bet pre-flop and has now c-bet 400 into a pot of 1200 (1/3 pot). This small sizing gives me excellent pot odds (400 to call into a total pot of 2000, requiring 20% equity). My gutshot (4 outs) and potential Ace outs (3 outs), combined with the advantage of position, give me sufficient equity to continue. Folding for this price would be too tight, and raising provides little utility against a strong 3-bet range. Calling is the standard play.

On the turn, I have improved to second pair with an Ace kicker. While I held the lead pre-flop and on the flop, the Ten of Diamonds is a card that provides some showdown value but doesn't necessarily beat the range of hands Pro would call a 3-bet and a flop c-bet with (likely Jacks, or occasionally lower pairs and draws). By checking, I control the pot size and can see how Pro reacts. If Pro checks back, I can likely take the hand to showdown or evaluate on the river. If Pro bets, I have a reasonable hand to consider calling depending on the sizing, as I now beat some of their semi-bluffs or smaller pairs.

On the Turn, the board is [3c 4d Jc Td]. The opponent checks to me. I hold [As 2s] giving me Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). The opponent's check after 3-betting pre-flop and c-betting the flop suggests they may be slowing down with a medium strength hand or giving up on a bluff. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed high-card hands, and my gutshot gives me equity to improve on the river. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands (pairs, strong draws), forcing me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see the river card, and potentially win at showdown or hit my straight.

On the river, the board is 3c-4d-Jc-Td-9d. I hold middle pair with Tens and an Ace kicker. Both players checked the turn, which suggests that neither of us likely holds a monster. My hand has decent showdown value, but there are several hands in Pro's range that could beat me, including any Jack, a straight (like Q8 or 87, though less likely), or a backdoor diamond flush. By checking, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown and control the size of the pot. If Pro checks back, I can see the showdown; if Pro bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing to determine if it's a value bet or a bluff, but checking is the best initial move to realize my hand's value.

I hold Ace-high (As2s) on a very coordinated board of [3c 4d Jc Td 9d]. The river card brings a third diamond (flush potential) and four-to-a-straight on the board. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but has since checked the turn and river. While my Ace-high has very little showdown value against a typical 3-bet range (losing to any pair and even AK/AQ high), bluffing this river is risky. The board texture connects heavily with a 3-bettor's range (sets, straights, high pairs), and although the opponent has shown weakness by checking, they may be exercising pot control with a hand like Overpairs (QQ/KK) or Top Pair (AJ) that they won't fold to a standard bet. Since I am in position with the chip lead, preserving my stack is preferable to firing a low-probability bluff into a wet board where I likely have little fold equity against the top of their checking range. I will check back and take the free showdown or accept the loss to keep variance low.

I am on the button with Qs3s. Looking at the history, Pro has folded to my 3x pre-flop raises from the small blind in hands #4, #6, #8, #10, #12, #14, and #20. This indicates a high fold-to-steal frequency from Pro. Qs3s is a strong enough speculative hand to open in heads-up play, and given the established pattern of success with this bet sizing, I will raise to 300.

The opponent is in the Small Blind (Button) and has raised to 300 (3bb). Based on hand history, the opponent appears to be opening 100% of their buttons for 3x, having done so in every opportunity (10/10 times). While J7o has decent raw equity (~43%) against a 100% range, the large 3x sizing offers poor pot odds (requiring ~33% equity) compared to a min-raise. Being out of position with a disconnected, offsuit hand makes realizing that equity difficult, especially against an opponent who has shown a tendency to c-bet. A conservative realization factor (e.g., 70-75%) puts the EV of calling slightly negative or break-even at best. Folding is the prudent play to avoid bleeding chips in a bloated pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I hold 2h 5c on the button (Small Blind). This is statistically one of the worst starting hands in heads-up Texas Hold'em. My previous play history shows I have been disciplined in folding weak hands like J2o and K3o from this position. With no potential for a straight or flush draw that isn't easily dominated, and given the opponent's tendency to defend or 3-bet, attempting to steal the blinds or limp is not profitable. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with 5c 6s. Looking at the match history, Pro is folding the big blind at a very high frequency when I raise (Hands 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 20, and 22). Even though 56o is a relatively weak hand, the statistical trend of the opponent folding to pre-flop aggression makes this a profitable spot to continue applying pressure. I will raise to my standard 3x sizing of 300.

I hold 2h 8d in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. My opponent, Flash, has been opening to 300 consistently (almost 100% frequency) from the button. While their range is likely very wide, 2h 8d is one of the worst starting hands (unsuited, disconnected, low rank). Against a 3x raise, I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600 total), requiring ~33% equity. Although raw equity vs a random hand is close to 32%, realizing this equity out of position with such a weak hand is extremely difficult. The standard play with 82o against a 3x open is to fold.

I hold 3d 2c (offsuit) in the Small Blind, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having the Button (position post-flop), the equity of this hand is too low to justify entering the pot, especially against an opponent who has demonstrated a willingness to defend their Big Blind and 3-bet. Attempting to steal the blinds with this hand has a negative expected value compared to folding. Therefore, the correct action is to fold immediately.

I am on the button with QJo, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent has been folding frequently to my 3x opens. I will continue with my strategy of raising to 300 to either take down the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage.

The opponent (Flash) has established a pattern of consistently opening to 3bb (300 chips) from the button. Facing a 3x raise while out of position, I need a stronger hand to defend profitably than I would against a min-raise. Although T6o has about 46% raw equity against a random hand, it suffers significantly from poor playability (poor equity realization) out of position. I need 25% realized equity to call, but T6o is easily dominated and hard to play post-flop. Given the larger raise size and the weak holdings, the correct move is to fold.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) on the button (Small Blind), which is a premium hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the opponent's random hand distribution. Looking at my history, I have frequently min-raised to 200 with a mixed range of hands (both value like Ac7c and bluffs/semi-bluffs like 8c5c). Raising to 200 here is standard to build the pot for value while balancing my opening range. I want to extract value from worse Ax, Kx, and Qx hands that the Big Blind might defend with.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro has opened with a minimum raise to 200. With 100 already committed as the big blind, I only need to call another 100 to see the flop, giving me excellent pot odds of 3:1 (100 to win a total pot of 400). T9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button open in heads-up play, despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The flop of Kd-3h-6c is quite dry and favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro), which contains many more Kings than mine. I have no pair and no real draw other than some backdoor straight potential. However, it is standard to check to the aggressor in this position. I will check and see how Pro reacts to this board.

The board is K-3-6 rainbow, which is very dry. I hold Ace-Queen, giving me strong showdown value but no pair. In Heads-Up, this is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' situation. If I bet, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (Kings, pairs of 6s or 3s, or pocket pairs) and fold out hands I am already crushing (lower unpaired hands). Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, keep the opponent's range wide (including dominated Ace-highs and random bluffs), and realize my equity. It also protects my checking range, making me harder to exploit on turns. I plan to call a turn bet or delay c-bet if checked to again.

I am out of position with Ten-high and no draw. The Ace on the turn is a very strong card for Pro's pre-flop raising range. Since the flop went check-check, it's possible Pro was pot-controlling a King or has now picked up equity/pairs with the Ace. I have no reason to bet here as a bluff into a range that contains many Aces and Kings, and I certainly cannot call a bet. I will check and hope to see a free river or fold if faced with aggression.

I checked back the flop with Ace-high to control the pot, and the turn Ace improves my hand to Top Pair with a Queen kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. My check on the flop under-represented my hand, so betting now serves two purposes: extracting value from King-x hands that might not believe I hit the Ace (or view a delayed bet as a bluff attempt) and getting value from worse Ace-x hands that defended the Big Blind. A half-pot bet of 200 is a good size to keep his calling range wide, including second-pair hands.

I am holding Ten-high with no pair and no draw on a board that features both an Ace and a King. Pro raised pre-flop, checked the King-high flop, and is now betting on the Ace turn. This 'delayed continuation bet' on a card that heavily favors their perceived range is very difficult to combat with a hand as weak as mine. I have no showdown value and no equity to improve. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 9h 3s. While this is a weak hand, the opponent (Pro) has shown an extremely high fold-to-steal frequency, folding to my 300-chip raises in 10 out of the last 12 instances when they were in the Big Blind. To break even on a 300-chip raise (risking 250 to win 150), I need the opponent to fold roughly 62.5% of the time. Since they are currently folding over 80% of the time, raising is a highly profitable exploit regardless of my hole cards.

I hold ATs (AcTc) in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). We are playing deep-stacked (~97 BBs). ATs is a very strong hand heads-up, well ahead of the opponent's wide opening range (which appears to be nearly 100% frequency based on recent history). While a 3-bet for value is a viable option, calling is preferable here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue with dominated hands; 2) It avoids bloating the pot out of position against a deep stack; 3) ATs plays very well post-flop. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

I am out of position with Ace-Ten suited (two overcards) on a low, somewhat coordinated board (4h 8s 6h). The board texture connects moderately well with a Big Blind defending range, but also hits the Button's opening range (overpairs, sets, flush draws). As the pre-flop caller, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as I have showdown value with Ace-high but cannot sustain multiple streets of aggression, and I don't want to fold out the opponent's bluffs or weaker hands that I currently beat. I will check to see how the opponent proceeds.

On the flop of 4h-8s-6h, the board is quite coordinated, but I maintain the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Although 9h 3s is a weak hand, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a backdoor heart draw. The opponent has shown a high tendency to fold when facing post-flop pressure in previous hands. By betting 300 into a 600 pot (a standard 50% pot continuation bet), I can capitalize on my 'fold equity' and likely take the pot down immediately if they haven't connected with this middle-heavy board.

I hold Ace-Ten suited (AcTc) on a low flop of 4h 8s 6h. Having checked, I face a half-pot continuation bet of 300 from the Button. Given the opponent's very wide opening range (nearly 100%), my Ace-high is likely ahead of many of his holdings (random overcards, straight draws, pure bluffs). I have two live overcards and backdoor flush potential. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring only ~25% equity to continue, which I certainly have against a wide range. Raising would isolate me against better hands, while folding is too nitty given my showdown value and equity. The best play is to float (call) to see a turn card, re-evaluating if I improve or if the board texture changes.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Four of Spades (4s), creating a double flush draw texture (hearts and spades). I hold Ace-Ten high. Leading out (donking) into the pre-flop aggressor who bet the flop is not a standard line with this holding, as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. My goal is to realize my equity cheaply or induce the opponent to check back, allowing me to see a river card or win at showdown with Ace-high. Therefore, checking is the correct strategic play.

The turn card is the 4s, which pairs the bottom card of the flop. Pro has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. While I represent a strong range as the pre-flop aggressor, Pro's call on a coordinated board (4h-8s-6h) suggests they have connected with the middle of the deck (likely an 8, a 6, or a draw). My 9-high has very little showdown value and no significant draws. Since Pro has shown a tendency to be 'sticky' once they call a flop bet, a second barrel on the turn is risky and likely to be called by any pair or heart draw. I will check behind to keep the pot manageable and see the river for free, potentially evaluating a bluff if the board becomes more favorable or if Pro checks again.

I hold Ace-Ten suited (AcTc) on a final board of 4h 8s 6h 4s Qc. The opponent raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn. This turn check often caps the opponent's range, suggesting a marginal made hand (like a mid-pair) or a draw that decided to take a free card. The River Queen is an overcard that might connect with broadway floats, but many draws (hearts, spades, 5-7) have missed. Betting here has little utility: I would likely fold out hands I beat (King-high, missed draws) and get called only by better hands (pairs). My Ace-high has decent showdown value against the opponent's missed draws. Therefore, the best play is to check, hoping for a check-back to win at showdown or inducing a bluff that I can consider catching.

The river Queen is a significant overcard that likely didn't improve the Big Blind's range, which consists mainly of middle pairs (8x, 6x) and missed draws (57, 79, or hearts) given their call on the flop and check on the turn. My 9-high has zero showdown value, so checking is a guaranteed loss. By betting 800 into the 1200 pot, I am representing a strong range that includes top pair (Qx), overpairs, or potentially a slow-played set/trips (4x) from the turn. Given Pro's established tendency to fold to post-flop pressure in previous hands, this represents an ideal spot to capitalize on fold equity and take down the pot.

I hold Ace-Ten suited (AcTc) on a board of 4h 8s 6h 4s Qc. The action went: I called a pre-flop raise, called a flop c-bet, and checked the turn where the opponent checked back. The River Queen is a scare card that favors the opponent's range (broadway cards) more than mine. While the opponent's turn check indicates weakness or a draw, the substantial river bet of 800 into 1200 (after the Queen hits) represents a polarized range: either a Queen/strong hand or a missed draw bluff. Although I unblock missed flush draws (by holding clubs), the opponent has not shown significant river bluffing tendencies in previous hands (e.g., Hand #2 checked back river). The Queen connects with many hands that would check back the turn (QJ, QT, KQ). Without a specific read that the opponent over-bluffs rivers, Ace-high is not strong enough to call ~28% pot odds against a value-heavy line. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

In the Small Blind with Jd 5h at ~93bb deep. This hand (J5 offsuit) is in the bottom 25-30% of starting hands. While a wide opening range is common in Heads-Up, J5o is typically a fold or a mix, especially against an opponent who has shown the capability to 3-bet (Hand 15, Hand 21) and defend actively. I have previously folded hands like K3o and J2o, and J5o is structurally poor for post-flop playability. Folding saves chips and maintains a stronger opening range.

I am on the button with a suited gapper. While 6-2 suited is a weak hand, Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression. I will continue my strategy of applying pressure from the button by raising to 300, which has been my standard opening size.

The opponent 'Flash' has raised to 300 on the Button in every observed opportunity (15/15 hands), indicating an extremely wide opening range (likely near 100%). I hold Ac5d, which has approximately 59% equity against a random hand and dominates a 'raise any two' strategy. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to play a 600 pot), requiring roughly 33% equity realization. Despite being out of position, folding an Ace is exploitable against such a high frequency opener. While a 3-bet is a valid option to punish the wide range, calling is a lower-variance standard defense that keeps the opponent's weaker hands in the pot.

The flop is monotone (Kh 8h Th), which heavily favors ranges containing a heart. I hold Ac5d, giving me Ace-high with no heart and no straight draws. Against an opponent who opens 100% of buttons, their range contains many random hearts and pairs that connect here. Leading out (donk betting) serves no purpose as I have no value and little fold equity on this texture. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold given the lack of equity and playability.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three hearts and high cards (K-T-8), which connects well with a Big Blind defending range. Holding only six-high with no hearts (6d 2d), I have zero equity and no draw. While I have been aggressive, this board is too dangerous to bluff into without at least a backdoor draw or a high heart. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see how the turn develops without overcommitting with a hand that has no showdown value.

The opponent 'Flash' has a 100% opening frequency on the button but checked back on the monotone Kh 8h Th flop. This check-back signals weakness or a desire to control the pot, making it less likely he holds a strong flush or top pair. The turn card is the 4c, a complete brick that does not change the board texture. I hold Ace-high (Ac5d), which currently has decent showdown value against a 100% range that showed weakness on the flop. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff that might only fold out hands I already beat (lower high-cards) while getting called by any pair or heart draw. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or re-evaluate on the river. If the opponent bets the turn, I can comfortably fold given the dangerous board texture.

The turn 4c is a total brick that doesn't change the board texture. Pro has checked to me for a second time, which might indicate weakness, but this board is heavily coordinated and likely hits a large portion of a Big Blind defending range. Since I have six-high with no hearts and no draw, I have zero equity. In line with my previous assessment of this street, I will check back to see the river for free and avoid inflating the pot with a hand that cannot win a showdown or comfortably weather a check-raise.

I hold Ac5d on a board of Th 8h Kh 4c 8s. The opponent 'Flash' raised pre-flop (indicating a wide range) but checked back both the flop (monotone hearts) and the turn (brick). This passive line suggests he does not have a strong King, flush, or Ten, capping his range to weak pairs, air, or perhaps a trap. On this river, the board pairs the 8. My best hand is a pair of Eights with an Ace kicker (8-8-A-K-T). I beat all of his unpaired hands (Q-high, J-high) and chop with any other Ace-high hand. I only lose to a pocket pair or a hand that connected with the board (K, T, 8, 4). Betting achieves little because worse hands (air) will fold and better hands (pairs) will likely call. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply to realize my equity or induce a bluff from his missed draws. Given the showdown value of Ace-high, checking is the optimal play.

The board has paired on the river with an 8, and there are three hearts present. Pro has checked for the third consecutive time, suggesting they likely have a weak hand or a missed draw. Since my six-high has zero showdown value and cannot win against any of Pro's range that calls pre-flop, I must bet to have any chance of winning the pot. A half-pot bet of 300 is designed to look like a thin value bet from a King or a Ten, or perhaps a slow-played 8, putting pressure on Pro's Ace-high hands and low pocket pairs that have checked along.

The opponent 'Flash' is playing a hyper-aggressive strategy with a 100% pre-flop raise frequency. However, his checks on the monotone flop and brick turn strongly cap his range, making it unlikely he holds a King, Flush, or strong Ten. The river pairs the 8, leaving me with the best possible unpaired hand (Pair of 8s with A-K-T kickers). While I lose to any pocket pair or hand that connected (Tx, 4x), the opponent's range is saturated with missed random hands and air (Q-high, J-high, etc.). The small river bet of 300 into 900 gives me 4:1 pot odds, requiring only 20% equity to call. Given the opponent's aggressive nature, he is capable of stabbing with air here. I call to catch bluffs.

In heads-up play, K2s (suited King) is a hand with sufficient equity to play from the Button (Small Blind). While K2 is the lowest kicker, the suitedness adds playability and flush potential. Folding suited Kings on the button is generally too tight. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to apply pressure, potentially take down the pot pre-flop, or play the rest of the hand in position with initiative. Given the stack depth of nearly 100 BBs, a standard open raise is the most profitable line.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2d, one of the weakest possible hands. Pro has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call, 42o realizes its equity extremely poorly out of position, and I will likely face difficult decisions on most flops. Given the deep stacks and the fact that I have better opportunities to contest pots, I will make the disciplined fold here.

I am on the button with suited connectors (3d 4d). Based on the previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has a high folding frequency to pre-flop raises from the button. Raising to 300 has been a successful strategy to take down the blinds or set up a post-flop play. Even if called, the hand has some post-flop potential.

I hold 8d5c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My hand is very weak (trash offsuit) with poor playability out of position. While I get 3:1 pot odds requiring 25% equity, realizing that equity with 85o is difficult due to domination and lack of connectivity. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand against a 3bb open.

I hold 8d 2s on the button (Small Blind). This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (82o). Although I have position, the hand has very poor playability and equity. Against an opponent who has shown willingness to defend their Big Blind (calling or 3-betting), opening with this hand is -EV. Standard strategy dictates folding the bottom 10-20% of hands from the button, and this definitely falls into that category. I will fold.

In heads-up play, a hand like Queen-Five offsuit is strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the Big Blind. Based on previous hands, the standard opening raise of 300 has been effective in picking up the blinds or taking control of the pot early. I will maintain this consistency.

The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. This has been a consistent pattern for Flash, suggesting a wide opening range. However, J2o is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em with very poor playability, especially out of position. Even against a wide range, J2o struggles to realize its equity due to reverse implied odds and lack of connectivity. The pot odds require ~33% equity, and while raw equity vs a random hand is higher, the realizable equity is likely lower than the threshold needed to call a 3x raise OOP. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the Button (SB) with Q8o. In heads-up play, this is a playable hand, ranking around the top 43% of hands. It is generally too strong to fold. While limping is an option, raising is superior to take the initiative, deny equity to the Big Blind's random holdings, and build a pot in position. My standard opening size has been a min-raise to 200, which has been effective in previous hands (e.g., Hand #7 where I raised Q8o and won). Therefore, I will raise to 200.

KTo is a strong hand in heads-up play and easily defends against a min-raise from the button. With 3:1 pot odds, calling is a standard and profitable play to see a flop while maintaining a manageable pot size out of position.

I have King-high on a 9-7-3 board with no direct draw, but King-high still has some showdown value and backdoor potential against some of the button's range. The opponent (Pro) has not yet acted on this flop. As the Big Blind, I will check to the aggressor to see how they react to this board texture, which can be connected but is generally better for the caller's range if it contains medium connectors. Checking allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to call or fold based on the size of a potential continuation bet.

I hold Queen-Eight offsuit on a Flop of 9s-7s-3d. The board texture is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and straight possibilities, which connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range. My hand has only Queen-high showdown value and no significant draws. Betting here (c-betting) is risky because I likely have little fold equity against pairs or strong draws, and if called, I am often behind. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a pair on the turn, or get to showdown cheaply with Queen-high. It also keeps the pot small with a marginal hand.

I have turned top pair with a Ten kicker on a board that checked through on the flop. The King is a very strong card for my range. Since Pro checked back the flop, their range is likely weak or consists of marginal made hands and draws. I will lead out with a bet of 250 to extract value from second pairs, draws (especially the spade flush draw), and perhaps induce a mistake from a hand like Ace-high.

I hold Queen-Eight offsuit (Qd 8c) on a board of 9s-7s-3d-Kd. After I checked back the flop, the opponent leads out for 250 into a pot of 400 on the turn. The King is a card that connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (Kx hands), and my check-back on the flop signaled a lack of strength (likely capping my range). I currently have only Queen-high with no straight or flush draws (I have 3 diamonds total, which cannot become a flush on the river). Against a pair, I have at most 6 outs (approx 13% equity), which is insufficient to call a bet offering roughly 28% pot odds. Floating with just Queen-high is too loose here without a specific read. The disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the button with an Ace-high hand (Ah 4c). In a heads-up situation, this is a strong starting hand. My established table image involves consistently raising to 3x the big blind (300 chips total) from the button to exert pressure and build the pot. I will continue this strategy here.

I hold KdJd in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button/SB. My hand (KJs) is very strong heads-up, with approximately 60-62% equity against a standard wide button opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to seize initiative, calling is preferable here because: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, including many dominated hands (Kx, Jx). 2) I am out of position, and calling avoids bloating the pot pre-flop or facing a 4-bet. 3) The price is good (calling 200 to win a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds). I will call and evaluate the flop.

I hold Middle Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board (Ac Js 4s). The Ace favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised as I am unlikely to get value from worse hands and will fold out bluffs, while isolating myself against better hands (Ax). I plan to check and call a standard continuation bet, as my hand has significant showdown value and beats many of the opponent's semi-bluffs (flush draws) or smaller pairs.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Fours) on a board with an Ace, a Jack, and a Four. My opponent checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. This is a very strong hand, but there are potential draws on the board (spades and broadway cards). I want to bet here for value against weaker Aces, Jacks, or draws, and to protect my hand from free cards. A bet of half the pot (300 chips) is a standard size that allows me to build the pot while still being callable by second-best hands.

I hold Middle Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on an A-J-4 board. The Ace favors the opponent's range, but my hand is too strong to fold to a single standard c-bet (50% pot) in a heads-up scenario. The opponent's range is wide enough to include many weaker hands and bluffs (flush draws, straight draws, air) against which I have high equity. Calling 300 to win a pot of 900 offers 3:1 odds, requiring only 25% equity, which I comfortably exceed. I have 9-high on an Ace-King-Trey board. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, and I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. There is no reason to lead out here; checking is the standard play to see how the opponent reacts.

I hold QTo on a board of [3c Ac Kd]. This board texture hits my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser very well (strong Aces and Kings), but it also connects heavily with the Big Blind's defending range (Ax, Kx, flush draws). While a c-bet could fold out small pairs, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (Jack needed) and some showdown value with Queen-high. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands (Ax, Kx), which would force me to abandon my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see the turn, and potentially hit my straight or re-evaluate. I have checked back similar hands in previous spots (e.g., Hand #27 with Ace-high on K-high board) to pot control.

I am holding 9-high on a board that now features two Aces and a King. On the flop, both I and my opponent checked, which suggests that the Ace-King high board didn't significantly hit either of our ranges, or Pro is playing a strong hand slowly. The second Ace on the turn makes it even less likely for either of us to have an Ace. However, with no pair and no draw, I have zero equity to call a bet. Betting here as a bluff is an option, but given that Pro checked back on the flop, they might be willing to call with a King or a middle pair. I will check to see Pro's reaction and potentially see a free river card.

I hold QTo on a board of [3c Ac Kd As]. I checked back the flop to pot control and realize equity. The turn pairs the Ace. Flash checks again. My hand has Queen-high showdown value and a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the nuts). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, trying to fold out pocket pairs or weak Kings, but given the double broadway and paired board, it's risky to face a check-raise or get called by a sticky King or slow-played Ace. Checking back guarantees I see the river card for free, giving me a chance to hit my straight or win at showdown if the opponent has a busted draw. Checking minimizes variance and maximizes realized equity.

The board has completed with two pair: Aces and Threes with a King kicker (Ac-As-3c-3s-Kd). Since I am holding 9-high, my best five-card hand is simply the board. Unless my opponent holds an Ace for a full house (A-A-A-3-3) or a Three for a full house (3-3-3-A-A), we are going to chop this pot. Betting serves no purpose because the opponent is unlikely to fold a hand that is also playing the board, and I cannot beat a hand that improves upon the board. Checking is the most logical and safe action.

The board is [3c Ac Kd As 3s], which means the best five-card hand on the board is Two Pair (Aces and Threes) with a King kicker (A-A-3-3-K). My hand [Td Qs] does not improve this; I am 'playing the board'. I lose to any opponent holding a King, Ace, or Three. I chop with any other hand. Betting achieves little because better hands (Kx, Ax, 3x) will call, and chopping hands will likely call or fold (gaining me only half the pot I already own). Checking back ensures I realize my equity in a split pot without risking chips against a slow-played King or Ace.

I am on the button with Jc 5s. In a heads-up situation, J5o is a marginal hand but generally worth a standard open-raise or a limp to see a flop. I hold Jack of Hearts and Ten of Hearts (JTs) in the Big Blind. This is a premium suited connector with excellent playability and equity against a standard Button opening range. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

The flop comes [7s, Js, Kh]. I have flopped middle pair with a Ten kicker, plus a gutshot straight draw (Queen gives me the nuts) and a backdoor flush draw. This board texture (King-high, broadway cards) favors the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands (Kx, better Jx, sets) and fold out worse hands that might bluff. My hand has decent showdown value and playability. The standard line is to check to the raiser. If the opponent bets, I have an easy call given my pair and draws. If they check back, I can re-evaluate on the turn. Checking keeps the opponent's range wide.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker. The board has a King, which is in both of our ranges, but my c-bet frequency should remain high here to put pressure on 'Pro'. Pro checked to me, so I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips). This bet serves to get value from weaker hands like a 7 or pocket pairs, protects against overcards, and might win the pot immediately if Pro missed the flop entirely.

On a board of [7s Js Kh], holding Middle Pair (Jacks) is strong enough to defend against a standard continuity bet. The opponent's range is wide, including Kx (which beats me), better Jx, but also many flush draws (spades), straight draws (QT, T9), and lower pairs or air. Folding Middle Pair to a single bet on the flop would be too weak and exploitable given the opponent's aggressive profile. Raising would likely only isolate me against Kx or better Jx. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range in the pot and re-evaluate on the turn, while the cost (300 chips into a pot that will be 1200) offers good odds.

On the turn, the board reads [7s Js Kh Ts]. This card improves my hand significantly to Two Pair (Jacks and Tens), but it also brings in a very dangerous texture: there are now three spades for a possible flush, and the connected broadway cards (T, J, K) complete several straights (AQ, Q9). Given the pre-flop raise and flop c-bet from Flash, their range connects heavily with this board (containing flushes, straights, sets, and better two pairs like KJ/KT). Leading out (donk betting) has little merit as it likely only gets called by better hands or forces folds from bluffs I want to keep in. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size, induce bluffs, and evaluate the opponent's action on this scary board.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more dangerous with the Ten of Spades, completing potential flushes and straights (like Q9 or 89). While I have second pair (Jacks), the board texture [7s Js Kh Ts] hits many parts of Pro's calling range from the flop. Pro checked to me, and since my hand has some showdown value but is likely behind a King, a Ten, or a completed draw, checking back is the best way to control the pot size and see a river card for free.

The river is the 9h, making the final board [7s Js Kh Ts 9h]. This is a very dangerous board texture. Betting forces folds from worse hands (like missed draws) and gets called or raised by better hands. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff from missed draws.

On this highly coordinated board (7s Js Kh Ts 9h), my pair of Jacks is now quite weak. Any Queen or Eight makes a straight, and there are three spades on the board for a potential flush. After Pro checked to me on the river, there is no value in betting; I will only be called by hands that beat me (Kings, Tens, straights, or flushes) and I might be check-raised. I hold 6s 2s in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up scenario. Although suited, it has low connectivity and poor high-card value. I hold JTo on the button, which is a strong hand in a heads-up distribution (top 30%). I hold JTo on an A-K-2 rainbow board. While this board favors my pre-flop raising range, it also connects heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many Ax and Kx hands. On this Ace-King-Deuce-Eight board, I am still holding Ten-high with no draws. The board is Ace-King-Deuce-Eight-Six with no flush possible. I have Ten-high, which has virtually zero showdown value. However, the pre-flop aggressor has checked back on both the flop and the turn. The board is K-2-A-8-6. I hold Jack-high, which has virtually zero showdown value as it loses to any pair and even Queen-high. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. I have a Ten of Diamonds and a Six of Diamonds on a board of Jack of Spades, King of Clubs, and Queen of Clubs. This gives me an open-ended straight draw, as any Nine or Ace will complete a straight. The board [Js Kc Qc] is extremely coordinated and broadway-heavy, which interacts very strongly with the Big Blind's calling range (lots of Kx, Qx, Jx, Tx). On the turn, the 9c has completed my straight (9-10-J-Q-K). However, the board is extremely dangerous as there are now four clubs visible, meaning any single club in my opponent's hand makes a flush. The river completes a board with four clubs (Kc, Qc, 9c, Tc) and a straight (9-10-J-Q-K). I have the straight using the board, but I do not hold any clubs in my hand. The board has run out [Js Kc Qc 9c Tc], which puts a 9-through-K straight on the board. I hold middle pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a Q-8-5 board. My opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600).
